title: Woodland area by type
subtitle: Estimated coverage of [populated woodlands](#dod:populated-woodlands), [residential woodlands](#dod:residential-woodlands), [remote woodlands](#dod:remote-woodlands) and [wild woodlands](#dod:wild-woodlands).
caption:
Woodland area by type

Estimated coverage of populated woodlands, residential woodlands, remote woodlands and wild woodlands.

This collection of nine stacked area charts displays estimated woodland coverage by type from 10,000 BCE to 2017 across various regions and the world. The legend identifies four categories: wild woodlands in dark green, remote woodlands in medium green, residential woodlands in teal, and populated woodlands in tan. The "World" chart, with a vertical axis reaching 40 million square kilometers, shows wild woodlands forming a stable base of approximately 15 million square kilometers throughout the timeline. Above this, remote woodlands constitute the largest segment for most of history before declining sharply after 4,000 BCE. In the "Near East" chart, which has a scale up to 300,000 square kilometers, remote woodlands drop precipitously around 4,000 BCE, replaced almost entirely by an expansion of populated woodlands that dominates the area until 2017. Similarly, the "Europe" chart shows a significant reduction in remote woodlands starting around 4,000 BCE, with populated woodlands expanding to fill the gap. In contrast, the charts for "Africa" and "Latin America" show remote woodlands remaining the dominant category with minimal change until very recently, where a slight dip is visible. The "Asia" and "North America" charts, both scaled to 10 million square kilometers, depict a gradual decline in remote woodlands accompanied by a steady rise in populated and residential woodlands, particularly in the later years. "Oceania" and "Eurasia" follow similar patterns of remote woodland dominance with recent decreases. Across all regions, wild woodlands remain a relatively constant, low-lying band at the bottom of each stack.
